steps.title: Identify the subject and what it needs
steps.content: "Puerto Rico's museums" is a plural noun phrase that is the grammatical subject of the blank. It needs a finite main verb to form a complete independent clause. Without a main verb, the sentence is a fragment.

steps.title: Apply the finite verb rule — per-choice diagnosis
steps.content:
Only a finite verb (one that shows tense and can stand alone as a predicate) can complete the clause. Non-finite forms cannot serve as main verbs on their own.
  • Choice A — ‘to inform’ an infinitive. Infinitives are non-finite and cannot be the main verb of a clause. Eliminated.
  • Choice B — ‘having informed’ a perfect participle. Participles are non-finite; they function as modifiers, not predicates. Eliminated.
  • Choice C — ‘inform’ the base form used as a present-tense plural verb. It is finite, agrees with the plural subject "museums," and completes the sentence. Correct.
  • Choice D — ‘informing’ a present participle. Like all participles, it is non-finite without a helping verb and cannot anchor the main clause. Eliminated.
